ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Ribena brand syrup diluted drink; closest defensible match is blackcurrant drink (FOODON_03301710).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers blackcurrant nectar and drinks; used as closest defensible anchor for this diluted blackcurrant syrup cordial.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for diluted blackcurrant cordial; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one glass of approximately 200 ml of Ribena prepared from syrup, providing around 88 kcal and 21.4 g of sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Ribena (from syrup) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Ribena (from syrup), record ID D03050002. All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ribena from syrup in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 52 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: blackcurrant drink / fruit cordial with similar sugar content (~10g/100g) from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (21.4 g per 200 ml) / 100 = 11.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (10.45 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-driven sodium retention, despite very low sodium (2 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid Ribena from syrup; the 10.45 g/100 g sugar load exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold and independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, regardless of the beverage's low sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 10.45 g sugar/100 g and zero dietary fibre, producing rapid glucose absorption and acute postprandial glycaemic excursion.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ribena from syrup is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in any portion size; as a sugar-sweetened beverage with 10.45 g sugar/100 g and no fibre buffer, it causes rapid glucose absorption and should be replaced with sugar-free cordial alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with elevated triglycerides, increased visceral adiposity, and heightened cardiovascular risk independent of total caloric intake.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit or eliminate Ribena from syrup from their diet, as habitual sugar-sweetened beverage consumption raises triglycerides and cardiovascular risk; switching to water or unsweetened blackcurrant infusions is recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar content (10.45 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators with no offsetting anti-inflammatory nutrients present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Ribena from syrup; the high sugar load promotes systemic inflammation and no meaningful anti-inflammatory factor (omega-3, polyphenols, whole-food fibre, or anti-inflammatory micronutrients) is present to offset this risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ribena from syrup is a ready-to-drink blackcurrant-flavoured cordial beverage prepared by diluting concentrated Ribena blackcurrant syrup with water. The resulting drink is deep purple-red in colour with a sweet, tangy blackcurrant flavour and a thin, watery texture. It is widely consumed in Singapore and across Commonwealth countries as a refreshment, particularly among children and at social gatherings. Per 100 g, the drink provides 44 kcal, 10.7 g carbohydrate (of which 10.45 g is sugar), negligible protein (0 g), negligible fat (0 g), negligible dietary fibre (0 g), and very low sodium (2 mg). The high sugar content classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB). Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to rapid glucose absorption from liquid sugar with no fibre buffer. It is also of concern for hypertension (AHA 2018 SSB sugar gate) and cardiovascular disease (triglyceride elevation from habitual SSB consumption). The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low GI) based on analogous blackcurrant drink data from the University of Sydney GI Database, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 11.1 per 200 ml serving. Ribena syrup is an ultra-processed product; the diluted drink is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is not recommended as a regular beverage for any individual managing metabolic or cardiovascular conditions.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in Ribena?
Is Ribena from syrup suitable for hypertension?
What is the glycemic index of Ribena?
Can I drink Ribena if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (record D03050002) at confidence 0.95. Source match is exact. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for a comparable blackcurrant cordial drink, clearly documented.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero by formulation — this is a diluted fruit syrup beverage containing no protein-bearing ingredients. fat_g is confirmed zero by formulation — no fat-containing ingredients are present in this diluted blackcurrant cordial. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — the syrup preparation removes whole-fruit fibre. saturated_fat_g, cholesterol_mg set to 0.0 as confirmed zero for this plant-based, fat-free beverage. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this specific Ribena syrup preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D03050002 as of 2026. Note: Ribena syrup does contain added vitamin C in the commercial product, but the specific quantity per 100 g of the diluted drink is not reported in the HPB source and cannot be confirmed without a product-specific label; therefore vitamin_c_mg is set to -1 pending verification. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on blackcurrant drink data from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Ribena from syrup was found in the approved GI source. Glycemic load per 200 ml serving = 52 × 21.4 / 100 = 11.1.
